verdicts.notes: Everyday fruit vocabulary fits guided Level 1. The hash H1, spacing, and hash-style checks align with the Goal; different wording and the misspelled lowercase variant match, while missing, malformed, and setext variants fail for their declared checks.

Familiar reading vocabulary is appropriate for Level 1 and forms a distinct retry variant. Fixtures prove prose and spelling independence while exercising the required H1, space-after-hash, and hash-style failures through the real engine.

Simple weather vocabulary fits guided production and differs from the other retry variants. Capitalization and spelling changes remain matched, and the three declared Markdown failures return the intended feedback IDs.

The learning-domain phrase is familiar and short enough for Level 1. All prose-independent match cases pass, and missing H1, malformed spacing, and setext style are rejected for the declared structural reasons.

Daily-travel vocabulary is suitable for hidden-syntax Level 2 recall. The candidate grades only the recalled hash-heading grammar: alternate wording and spelling pass, while missing, malformed, and setext structures fail as specified.

The errands vocabulary is common US English and a distinct recall variant. Real-engine fixtures confirm that content is not graded and that each declared heading-syntax failure produces the intended result.

The daily-routine phrase fits Level 2 recall and broadens the retry pool without increasing syntax complexity. Alternate prose and typo fixtures match; missing H1, bad spacing, and setext form fail for the expected checks.

The everyday planning phrase is appropriate for recall and distinct from the other Level 2 variants. Fixture outcomes demonstrate case and spelling tolerance plus direct coverage of all three heading checks.

This is a readable workplace note with one H1, two H2 sections, bold emphasis in the first section, and a three-item unordered follow-up list in the second. Section-occurrence checks align with the target; swapped prose and lowercase typo variants pass, and every malformed positional variant fails for its declared check.

The decision-note scenario fits Level 3 human readability and provides a distinct workplace retry. The first H2 owns bold emphasis and the second owns the unordered action list by position, independent of section titles; all positive and negative real-engine fixtures behave as declared.

The onboarding guide is appropriate Level 3 workplace content with a clear scan-friendly hierarchy. Structural checks correctly bind bold content to H2 occurrence zero and the three-item bullet list to occurrence one, while arbitrary prose, case, and spelling remain ungraded.

The handoff scenario matches Level 3's human-readable document goal and completes a four-item retry family. The outline, hierarchy, exact H2 count, first-section emphasis, and second-section bullet checks all align with the target and have direct passing failure evidence.

The accessibility scenario is a credible executable development spec. Five H2 sections are checked in order; the third owns ordered implementation steps, the fourth unordered acceptance items, and the fifth one fenced language-tagged command block. Prose-independent positives and every direct positional failure pass through the real engine.

The bounded retry scenario and vocabulary fit Level 4 technical planning. H2 occurrence-based checks correctly distinguish ordered work, unordered acceptance criteria, and verification code without reading section prose; all alternate, malformed, and direct-check fixtures behave as declared.

This feature request is a suitable Level 4 executable spec with scope, dependencies, ordered implementation, acceptance criteria, and tagged verification commands. Target-to-check positions are correct, and the engine accepts unrelated prose and lowercased misspellings while rejecting every declared structural defect.

The regression-focused bug specification fits Level 4 and broadens the retry family. Its five H2 positions map correctly to the ordered implementation, unordered acceptance, and fenced verification checks; prose variations pass and each syntax mutation fails for the expected reason.

Revision 2 is grammar-only: it has no protected prose, document-length predicate, title-text operand, or spelling/case operand. The Goal has one H1, seven H2 sections, three H3 stages, correctly scoped authority, constraint, and stop lists, and fenced language-tagged bash and markdown blocks. All nine checks have isolated counterexamples, and the migration and rollback context is coherent.

Revision 2 carries no prose or length grading. Its anatomy is one H1, exactly seven H2 sections, three H3 stages, correctly scoped direct lists, and separate fenced verification and report blocks. All nine direct failure fixtures isolate their declared checks, and the security-sensitive compatibility and rollback context is credible.

Revision 2 grades Markdown anatomy only; changed prose and case/spelling fixtures remain Matched and no source-length limits are declared. The Goal has the required seven-section hierarchy, three stages, direct authority, constraint, and stop lists, plus the two tagged fenced blocks. All nine checks have direct counterexamples.

Revision 2 has empty protected prose and no prose-similarity or length predicate. Its AST contains one H1, exactly seven H2 sections, three H3 stages, correctly scoped direct lists, and fenced bash and markdown blocks. All nine direct fixtures return the declared first failure, and the release context forms a credible agent work order.
